verses.text: God sends Jehu the prophet against Baasha: lifted from the dust to rule, he walked in Jeroboam's sins, so his house too will be swept away. The pattern repeats — raised by God, then rebellious, then ruined. Men never learn from the judgments they themselves were sent to fulfill.

God sends Jehu the prophet against Baasha: lifted from the dust to rule, he walked in Jeroboam's sins, so his house too will be swept away. The pattern repeats — raised by God, then rebellious, then ruined. Men never learn from the judgments they themselves were sent to fulfill.

God sends Jehu the prophet against Baasha: lifted from the dust to rule, he walked in Jeroboam's sins, so his house too will be swept away. The pattern repeats — raised by God, then rebellious, then ruined. Men never learn from the judgments they themselves were sent to fulfill.

God sends Jehu the prophet against Baasha: lifted from the dust to rule, he walked in Jeroboam's sins, so his house too will be swept away. The pattern repeats — raised by God, then rebellious, then ruined. Men never learn from the judgments they themselves were sent to fulfill.

Baasha dies and Elah his son reigns, the sentence still hanging over the house. God's word, once spoken, waits patiently for its hour. Delay in judgment is not escape from it; the reckoning is only postponed, not cancelled.

Baasha dies and Elah his son reigns, the sentence still hanging over the house. God's word, once spoken, waits patiently for its hour. Delay in judgment is not escape from it; the reckoning is only postponed, not cancelled.

Baasha dies and Elah his son reigns, the sentence still hanging over the house. God's word, once spoken, waits patiently for its hour. Delay in judgment is not escape from it; the reckoning is only postponed, not cancelled.

Zimri, a captain, conspires and kills the drunken Elah, seizing the throne. The house that rose by murder falls by murder. Violence breeds violence; the throne grabbed with blood is seldom held for long.

Zimri, a captain, conspires and kills the drunken Elah, seizing the throne. The house that rose by murder falls by murder. Violence breeds violence; the throne grabbed with blood is seldom held for long.

Zimri, a captain, conspires and kills the drunken Elah, seizing the throne. The house that rose by murder falls by murder. Violence breeds violence; the throne grabbed with blood is seldom held for long.

Zimri wipes out all of Baasha's house, fulfilling Jehu's prophecy. Again the LORD's word is exactly performed through a ruthless hand. God's justice grinds slowly but finely; not one of His sentences fails to land.

Zimri wipes out all of Baasha's house, fulfilling Jehu's prophecy. Again the LORD's word is exactly performed through a ruthless hand. God's justice grinds slowly but finely; not one of His sentences fails to land.

Zimri reigns only seven days; when the army hears, they make Omri king and march on the city. The usurper's triumph lasts a week. Power seized against God's order is brittle, collapsing almost before it is grasped.

Zimri reigns only seven days; when the army hears, they make Omri king and march on the city. The usurper's triumph lasts a week. Power seized against God's order is brittle, collapsing almost before it is grasped.

Zimri reigns only seven days; when the army hears, they make Omri king and march on the city. The usurper's triumph lasts a week. Power seized against God's order is brittle, collapsing almost before it is grasped.

Seeing the city taken, Zimri burns the palace over himself and dies in the flames, for his sins. The seven-day king ends in self-destruction. A life that grasps everything by treachery often finds nothing left at the end but ashes.

Israel splits between Tibni and Omri, and Omri's faction prevails. Civil strife tears the kingdom further. A people who forsook God find even their own ranks divided, unable to agree or to rest.
Israel splits between Tibni and Omri, and Omri's faction prevails. Civil strife tears the kingdom further. A people who forsook God find even their own ranks divided, unable to agree or to rest.

Omri reigns, builds Samaria as his capital, and 'did worse than all that were before him,' deepening Jeroboam's idolatry. Each dynasty sinks lower than the last. Sin, unrepented, does not stay level; it descends, generation pulling generation further down.

Omri reigns, builds Samaria as his capital, and 'did worse than all that were before him,' deepening Jeroboam's idolatry. Each dynasty sinks lower than the last. Sin, unrepented, does not stay level; it descends, generation pulling generation further down.
Omri dies and Ahab his son reigns in his place. The throne passes to a man who will outdo even his father's evil. When a family will not turn, its worst is often yet to come.
Omri dies and Ahab his son reigns in his place. The throne passes to a man who will outdo even his father's evil. When a family will not turn, its worst is often yet to come.

Ahab does evil above all before him, marries Jezebel, and builds a temple and altar for Baal in Samaria, provoking the LORD more than all the kings of Israel. The nation reaches a new depth of apostasy. Married to idolatry, the kingdom now serves a foreign god outright.

Ahab does evil above all before him, marries Jezebel, and builds a temple and altar for Baal in Samaria, provoking the LORD more than all the kings of Israel. The nation reaches a new depth of apostasy. Married to idolatry, the kingdom now serves a foreign god outright.

In Ahab's days Hiel rebuilds Jericho, losing his firstborn and youngest sons, just as Joshua had cursed. God's ancient word lands centuries later, to the letter. No curse of God expires with time; His word stands sure across the generations.
